title: G7 Fractures on Trade and Tariffs as Oil Market Rebalances from Iran War
subhead: Trump's 100% wine tariff threat against France over digital services tax overshadows G7 unity, while oil markets price in a post-Hormuz world and central banks navigate inflation legacies from the Iran war.

sections.heading: Trump Threatens 100% Tariff on French Wine Over Digital Tax
sections.body:
President Trump opened the G7 summit in Evian by threatening a 100% tariff on French wine and champagne unless Paris removes its digital services tax targeting US technology firms. The threat, first reported by the New York Post on June 15 and repeated at the summit, would override a just-finalized US-EU trade deal, European officials said.

French President Macron rebuffed the demand publicly, stating 'the US does not decide European law' and insisting France's digital services tax — which applies to companies like Apple, Amazon, and Meta — is a matter of sovereign fiscal policy. The confrontation pits Trump's America First trade doctrine against European digital regulation, turning a gathering meant to showcase allied unity into a showcase of transatlantic economic friction.

Trump expanded the threat beyond France, saying any country implementing a digital services tax on US tech firms would face the same 100% tariff, effectively weaponizing US market access against a policy now adopted or proposed by over a dozen countries.
sections.whyItMatters: The tariff threat undermines the recently finalized US-EU trade agreement and signals that digital sovereignty disputes are now a primary axis of US-European economic conflict, distinct from traditional goods trade.

sections.heading: Oil Markets Price In Post-Hormuz World as Demand Forecasts Diverge Sharply
sections.body:
Crude prices fell 5% to a three-month low this week on hopes that the US-Iran framework deal will reopen the Strait of Hormuz, with Reuters reporting that spot premiums have fallen to pre-war levels. But the supply picture is far from settled: mine-clearing operations in the Strait could delay full shipping resumption by 40–50 days, according to military assessments.

The demand side tells a different story. The IEA has dramatically revised its 2026 oil demand outlook, now forecasting a year-over-year contraction of about 420,000 barrels per day — a swing of over 1.3 million bpd from its pre-conflict projection of 930,000 bpd growth. OPEC's forecast remains more optimistic at 970,000 bpd growth, a gap that reflects fundamental disagreement about whether the Iran war's demand destruction is permanent.

European aviation fuel supply offers a microcosm: Middle East refineries previously supplied roughly a quarter of Europe's jet fuel. The crisis forced US refiners to raise jet fuel's share of output from 10.5% to 12.7%, producing roughly 250,000 more barrels per day. Airlines averted summer shortages, but at premium prices that doubled to over $230 per barrel in April before falling to around $141 by late May, according to CNA reporting.
sections.whyItMatters: The divergence between IEA and OPEC demand forecasts is unusually wide, and the path from supply shock to potential surplus could be rapid — creating a boom-bust cycle in oil that punishes both producers and consumers in different phases.

sections.heading: BOJ at 31-Year High, Yen Under Pressure as Takaichi and Markets Clash
sections.body:
The Bank of Japan raised its benchmark rate to 1% on June 16, a 31-year high, up from 0.75%, citing the weak yen and inflationary pressures from the Iran war's oil shock. Governor Ueda, recently hospitalized, did not attend the policy board meeting, and the BOJ warned that Middle East developments, FX movements, and 'global AI-related demand' all require close monitoring.

Despite the hike, the yen remains under severe pressure, trading at 161.2 per dollar on Friday after hitting a 40-year low of 162.84 on Tuesday. Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama issued a fresh warning on July 4, saying Tokyo was in regular contact with Washington on FX issues and remained 'ready to respond appropriately at any time.' A government panel member and former dove, Toshihiro Nagahama, publicly called for 'moderate BOJ rate hikes' to rectify 'excessive yen weakness.'

The policy crosscurrents are stark: 10-year JGB yields hit a 30-year high on Friday, as investors interpreted PM Takaichi's economic blueprint as signaling substantial new spending and resistance to further BOJ hikes. Meanwhile, Japan's 2026 wage negotiations concluded with average gains above 5% for a third consecutive year, according to Bloomberg — a sign that the inflation-wage spiral the BOJ has long sought may be entrenched.

Yen-driven bankruptcies rose 32.3% year-on-year in the first half of 2026, with 45 companies collapsing under import cost pressures, according to Tokyo Shoko Research.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is caught between fiscal expansion and monetary tightening, with the yen near historic lows despite rates at three-decade highs. The wage-price spiral is real, but political pressure against further hikes is intensifying — a recipe for policy paralysis and continued yen weakness.

sections.heading: ECB Holds at 2.25% After June Hike, But Hawks Demand More
sections.body:
The European Central Bank raised its deposit facility rate to 2.25% on June 11 — its first hike since 2023 — driven by energy inflation from the Iran war. ECB Governing Council member Emmanuel Moulin said on July 3 that the bank is in a 'good position' after the move, crediting the decline in oil prices following the US-Iran deal.

But the hawks are not satisfied. Executive Board member Isabel Schnabel warned on June 25 that the US-Iran deal 'won't resolve eurozone inflation,' arguing that damage to energy infrastructure will keep price pressures elevated. ECB Governing Council member Madis Kaasik has suggested another rate hike is likely in July. Belgium's Pierre Wunsch also kept a July hike 'in play.' Chief Economist Philip Lane acknowledged the Iran deal was 'welcome' but said the ECB may still need to raise rates further if inflation evidence demands it.

Adding weight to the hawkish case: French inflation fell back to the ECB's 2% target in June for the first time this year, pulling back from a two-year high. But ECB Governing Council member Gabriel Makhlouf warned that price pressures may linger due to 'damaged energy assets,' and Spanish HICP inflation remained at 3.6%, well above target, suggesting the disinflation story is uneven across the eurozone.
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB's single hike is a down payment, not a conclusion. With hawks already positioning for a July move and inflation dynamics highly uneven across member states, the bank faces a classic one-size-fits-all dilemma — and the Iran war's energy legacy makes it worse.

sections.heading: Japan Pushes Critical Minerals Stockpiling at G7 as China Restricts Rare Earths
sections.body:
Prime Minister Takaichi announced she would propose joint stockpiling of rare earths and other critical minerals at the G7 summit, aiming to coordinate allied action against China's use of mineral supply chains as strategic leverage. Japan already operates a national stockpiling regime and plans to support other major countries in adopting similar systems.

The proposal comes as China has imposed continued export restrictions on rare earths to Japan following Takaichi's suggestion last year that Japan could intervene in a Taiwan contingency. Canada and Japan are also working on potential joint mining projects, including critical mineral stockpiling, Canada's trade minister told Reuters.

However, Japan's approach creates friction with some G7 partners. The Trump-Xi summit last month set a conciliatory tone in US-China relations, and Britain and Germany have been deepening economic cooperation with Beijing. Japan has also launched a survey for critical mineral mining in Greenland, the Nikkei reported.

Separately, Trump's own critical minerals pricing plan faces skepticism from G7 allies and a divided industry, with some partners wary of US efforts to set commodity prices by fiat.
sections.whyItMatters: Rare earth supply chains are the next arena of great-power competition after semiconductors. Japan's push for coordinated allied stockpiling is pragmatic, but it runs directly into the contradiction of some G7 members deepening China trade ties while others restrict them.

sections.heading: Iran's $12 Billion Frozen Assets Demand Shapes Nuclear Deal Implementation
sections.body:
Iran's chief negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf claimed that arrangements have been finalized for the release of $12 billion in frozen Iranian assets during talks with US officials in Switzerland. Iranian media reported that Tehran seeks a total of $24 billion, with $12 billion released upon signing and another $12 billion within 60 days.

US officials have not confirmed those figures. Vice President Vance described the negotiations as 'the foundation, not the house,' emphasizing Washington would judge by actions rather than promises. Vance said unfrozen assets could be used to purchase US goods like soybeans — a claim Iran's UN ambassador Ali Bahreini flatly rejected, insisting 'Iran is the only country who will decide what to do with its assets.'

President Trump separately denied reports that the US would invest money in Iran, calling a $300 billion fund report 'ridiculous.' The framework deal signed between the US and Iran includes a 60-day ceasefire but leaves major issues — including the scope of oil sales permitted and the mechanism for asset release — to future negotiations.
sections.whyItMatters: The frozen assets dispute reveals the fundamental implementation gap in the Iran deal: both sides publicly disagree on who controls unfrozen funds and how they can be spent. This disagreement could derail the 60-day ceasefire window before it expires.

sections.heading: UK-Japan £18 Billion Deal and European Defense Spending Surge Signal Geopolitical Realignment
sections.body:
The UK and Japan signed a landmark £18 billion ($24 billion) investment and technology partnership during PM Takaichi's visit to Downing Street ahead of the G7 summit. The deal includes a £9 billion boost to offshore wind projects and a technology partnership focused on next-generation capabilities and defense cooperation. Japan's Nissan is also in advanced talks with the UK government for financial support in exchange for a long-term commitment to its Sunderland plant.

The investment deal accompanies a broader European defense spending surge: the UK announced a £298 billion defense investment plan, and Germany and Spain launched 'Team Gen 6' after the FCAS fighter program collapsed, reflecting the fragmentation of European defense procurement. France shut down Israeli company stands at the Eurosatory defense exhibition.

Japan also remains undecided on whether to send military forces to the Strait of Hormuz, with PM Takaichi pushing for a coordinated G7 response to maritime security rather than unilateral national deployments.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK-Japan deal is a concrete example of how geopolitical risk is driving new investment alignments — a trade and technology pact between two island nations seeking strategic depth. The collapse of FCAS and rise of alternative defense partnerships shows how security fragmentation creates economic winners and losers.

bottomLine: The G7 summit in Evian opened with a transatlantic trade war threat (Trump's 100% wine tariff over France's digital tax) hanging over discussions meant to project allied unity. Meanwhile, the Iran war's financial legacy is still being priced in: oil markets are rebalancing from supply shock toward potential surplus, the BOJ and ECB are navigating inflation at 31-year and multi-decade rate highs respectively, and the yen sits near 40-year lows despite Tokyo's intervention warnings. The structural story — dedollarization, minerals competition, defense spending — continues to unfold through concrete deals like the UK-Japan pact and Japan's rare earth stockpiling proposal, not through abstract frameworks.
sources: AP News, Reuters, Bloomberg, CryptoBriefing, MSN, Financial Express, IB Times, Channel News Asia, Firstpost, Herald Business (Korea), Straits Times, Irish Times, Star Advertiser (Honolulu), Yomiuri Shimbun, Nikkei, Wall Street Journal, White House press reports
